summary: Jeff Atwood highlights KeyTraino, a concept originated by Leon Bambrick that teaches developers keyboard shortcuts by showing them whenever they use mouse-based navigation. SlickEdit released a free Visual Studio 2005 add-in called Command Spy that implements this idea, tracking command execution and revealing associated key bindings. The add-in also includes whimsical features like the ability to add dancing banana graphics to the editor. The post is a brief endorsement of tools that help developers become more efficient through keyboard mastery.
key_points: KeyTraino shows keyboard shortcuts when users click menus or toolbars, teaching them faster alternatives
SlickEdit's Command Spy add-in tracks all executed commands and their associated key bindings
The tool helps developers learn shortcuts organically through their normal workflow
The add-in includes humorous extras like embedding custom graphics in the IDE
money_quotes: When you use the toolbar, the menus or the context-menus of an application, KeyTraino shows the alternative keystroke you could've used.
The main purpose of this tool is to allow you to learn what commands are bound to which keystrokes, so that you can work faster within the IDE.
key_insight: The best way to learn keyboard shortcuts is through passive reinforcement during normal work, not through memorization.
